activities_projects.title: The First Rulers of Delhi
activities_projects.objective: Identify the early dynasties that made Delhi their capital
steps: Read the section on the Tomara Rajputs and Chauhans
Identify which ruler first made Delhi his capital (Anangapala)
Write down the name of the famous Chauhan ruler (Prithviraj Chauhan)
Identify the year when the Turkish Sultans took over Delhi (1192)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the transition of Delhi from a Rajput capital to the seat of the Sultanate
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of early dynasties and rulers (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Dynasty Timeline Strip
activities_projects.objective: Visualize the five dynasties of the Delhi Sultanate
steps: List the five dynasties: Early Turkish, Khalji, Tughluq, Sayyid, and Lodi
Draw a timeline and place them in the correct order
Color each dynasty section differently
Mark the total time period from 1206 to 1526
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the chronological sequence of the Sultanate rulers
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of the sequence and time periods (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Raziyya Sultan: The Brave Queen
activities_projects.objective: Understand the challenges faced by a female ruler
steps: Find the name of Iltutmish’s daughter (Raziyya)
Identify the year she became Sultan (1236) and when she was removed (1240)
Write one reason why people (the chronicler Minhaj-i Siraj) felt uncomfortable with her
List one quality that made her more capable than her brothers
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the social and political barriers for women in the medieval period
activities_projects.evaluation: Understanding of Raziyya's significance and the reasons for her removal (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Tarikh and Tawarikh Sources
activities_projects.objective: Identify how history was written in the Sultanate
steps: Identify the language in which 'Tawarikh' was written (Persian)
List who wrote these (secretaries, administrators, poets)
Understand that these authors lived in cities and wrote for the Sultan
Write a 'Diary Entry' of a court poet praising a Sultan’s victory
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the primary literary sources used to study the Delhi Sultanate
activities_projects.evaluation: Knowledge of the language and authors of historical records (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Garrison Towns to Empire
activities_projects.objective: Learn how the Sultans expanded their control
steps: Define a 'Garrison Town' (a fortified settlement with soldiers)
Understand the difference between the 'Hinterland' and the 'Garrison Town'
Draw a simple fort representing the Garrison and fields representing the Hinterland
List one Sultan who expanded the empire to South India (Alauddin Khalji)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains how the Sultanate moved from small outposts to a large empire
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of the drawing and definition of key terms (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Circle of Justice
activities_projects.objective: Understand the link between the King and the People
steps: Draw a circle and write the stages: King $ightarrow$ Soldiers $ightarrow$ Salaries $ightarrow$ Revenue $ightarrow$ Peasantry $ightarrow$ Justice $ightarrow$ King
Explain why soldiers need peasants (for salaries from revenue)
Explain why peasants need the King (for justice and protection)
Write one sentence on why this circle is important
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes the interdependence of various groups in a medieval state
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct representation of the 'Circle of Justice' (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Iqta and Iqtadar
activities_projects.objective: Learn about the administration of land
steps: Define 'Iqta' (land grant) and 'Iqtadar' or 'Muqti' (holder of the land)
List two duties of a Muqti (lead military campaigns, maintain law and order)
Note how the Muqtis were paid (from the revenue of their Iqtas)
Write that Iqtadars were frequently transferred so they didn't become too powerful
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the administrative system used to manage the Sultanate's lands
activities_projects.evaluation: Definition and role of the Iqtadar/Muqti (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Masjid: The Place of Prostration
activities_projects.objective: Understand the importance of the Mosque
materials: Pictures of Quwwat al-Islam or Moth ki Masjid
steps: Define the word 'Masjid' (Place where a Muslim prostrates in prayer)
Identify what the 'Qibla' is (direction towards Mecca)
Learn that Muslims in India face West to find the Qibla
Name one mosque built during the Tughluq period (e.g., Begumpuri Mosque)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Learns the basic terminology and architectural sites of the Sultanate period
activities_projects.evaluation: Identification of mosque components and examples (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Comparing Khalji and Tughluq
activities_projects.objective: Identify differences in how rulers handled problems
steps: Create a simple table with columns: 'Alauddin Khalji' and 'Muhammad Tughluq'
Compare them on 'Mongol Attacks' (Khalji built Siri; Tughluq moved people to Daulatabad)
Compare them on 'Salaries' (Khalji paid in cash; Tughluq used 'token' currency)
Note which ruler’s administrative measures were considered failures (Tughluq)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates between the administrative styles of two major Sultans
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct points in the comparison table (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The End of the Sultanate
steps: Identify the two dynasties after the Tughluqs (Sayyid and Lodi)
Find out the name of the last Sultan of Delhi (Ibrahim Lodi)
Identify the year he was defeated (1526) in the Battle of Panipat
Write who defeated him (Babur, the first Mughal)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands how the Delhi Sultanate ended and the Mughal Empire began
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of the names and dates for the end of the Sultanate (10 marks)
